abstract: While the excellence of Martin’s introduction is remarkably sustained, its impact is dampened by two specific lapses. First, the assertion that a BA degree entitled Marlowe to style himself "Sir Christopher Marlowe" is fundamentally misleading. While Martin follows Constance Kuriyama’s 2002 biography on this point, the title in question was a localized academic convention—the English equivalent of the Latin *Dominus*—rather than a mark of social rank. As evidenced by the figure of Sir Topas in *Twelfth Night*, such a designation did not elevate an individual to the social standing of a knight or baronet. Second, it is unnecessarily dismissive to categorize scholarly arguments for Marlowe’s death as a political assassination as "conspiracy theories." While one may certainly contest the conclusions of researchers like David Riggs, applying such a reductive label serves to stifle academic debate rather than engage with it rigorously.
